coredata.dc:description: The path and mechanism of charge transfers in composite catalyst are important for degrading organic pollutants effectively. A quasi-ternary Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with carbon quantum dots (CQD), CdS, and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (CQD/CdS/g-C3N4) has been successfully constructed in this work, and its electron transfer path is elucidated by contrast CdS/CQD/g-C3N4 photocatalyst. The built-in electric field at the interface between CdS and g-C3N4 induces exciton separations. The energy barrier between CQD and CdS accelerated charge transfers and further prevents exciton annihilation. As a result, CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 exhibits the greatest photocatalytic degradation, and the removal rates of Rhodamine B and sulfadiazine are 94.7 % and 76.0 %, which are 2.46 times and 1.47 times of CdS/CQD/g-C3N4, 7.02 times and 7.92 times of g-C3N4, respectively. It is further proved that CQD as the active site can promote the generation of H2O2 and effectively inhibit the formation of •O2 – in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, the degradation pathway and products of sulfadiazine are explored by means of HPLC-MS and calculation of Gaussian software. This work demonstrates the relationship between photocatalytic activity and electron transfer path, meanwhile, provides a reference for the design of efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: A CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for dye and antibiotic degradation: Dual carrier driving force and tunable electron transfer pathway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photogenerated transfer and catalytic reaction mechanism in CQD/CdS/g-C3N4.

The path and mechanism of charge transfers in composite catalyst are important for degrading organic pollutants effectively. A quasi-ternary Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with carbon quantum dots (CQD), CdS, and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (CQD/CdS/g-C3N4) has been successfully constructed in this work, and its electron transfer path is elucidated by contrast CdS/CQD/g-C3N4 photocatalyst. The built-in electric field at the interface between CdS and g-C3N4 induces exciton separations. The energy barrier between CQD and CdS accelerated charge transfers and further prevents exciton annihilation. As a result, CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 exhibits the greatest photocatalytic degradation, and the removal rates of Rhodamine B and sulfadiazine are 94.7 % and 76.0 %, which are 2.46 times and 1.47 times of CdS/CQD/g-C3N4, 7.02 times and 7.92 times of g-C3N4, respectively. It is further proved that CQD as the active site can promote the generation of H2O2 and effectively inhibit the formation of •O2 – in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, the degradation pathway and products of sulfadiazine are explored by means of HPLC-MS and calculation of Gaussian software. This work demonstrates the relationship between photocatalytic activity and electron transfer path, meanwhile, provides a reference for the design of efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of human society, industrial wastewater causes different degrees of environmental pollution and is more and more difficult to be degraded by nature. The main pollutants in wastewater are the colorful dyes from the textile industry and broad- and narrow-spectrum antibiotics from the pharmaceutical industry. One way to solve the environmental crisis is to use semiconductor photocatalysis. However, the traditional one-component semiconductor photocatalyst has been unable to meet the needs of photocatalytic requirements due to its limitations, such as low spectral utilization rate [1], high photogenerated carrier recombination rate [2], and almost no active site [3]. It is urgent to develop new and efficient photocatalysts to solve these problems. Many feasible methods have been proposed, such as micro-nano structure regulating [4–9], semiconductor coupling [10–12], element doping, etc. [13–16]. Compared to other methods, the strategies of micro-nano structure regulating can effectively prolong the exciton annihilation time, and semiconductor coupling share a unique mechanism for improving the activity by regulating electron transfers.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted extensive attention due to its good chemical and thermal stability, being free of metal elements, suitable band gap (2.78 eV), and environmental friendliness. However, bulk g-C3N4 has some serious disadvantages, such as fast electron-hole recombination speed and chaotic electron migration direction, which will inevitably lead to the reduction of photocatalytic activity. The simplest and most effective solution is to construct a two-dimensional (2D) layered nanosheet structure [10,14–20]. The nanoscale thickness can make the material generate carriers rapidly and promote the separation of excitons, thus realizing rapid transfers of free charges [21,22]. However, a common issue with 2D materials lies in the fact that the produced exciton cannot break through its own bondage, which will shorten the lifetime of the carrier and reduce the photocatalytic performance.

A feasible method to solve the above problem is to form heterojunctions through semiconductor coupling. The heterojunction can not only broaden the frequency band of light response, but also promote the drift motion of carriers through its unique built-in electric field. At present, several photocatalytic binary heterojunctions based on g-C3N4 have been reported, including TiO2/g-C3N4 [23–25], WO3/g-C3N4 [26], CsPbBr3/g-C3N4 [27], ZnO/g-C3N4 [28], CuS/g-C3N4 [29,30], and SnS2/g-C3N4 [31,32]. The design of g-C3N4 heterostructures require a comprehensive analysis of the band structure of other components.

CdS is well matched with g-C3N4, i.e., its conduction band (CB) is located lower than that of g-C3N4 and its valence band (VB) is located more positive than that of g-C3N4. One-dimensional (1D) CdS can make the photogenerated electrons and holes move in time and efficiently, prolonging the life of the photogenerated carriers, and result in a higher photocatalytic activity [33–35]. Therefore, it is a wise choice to construct binary heterojunctions with 2D g-C3N4 and 1D CdS. At present, most reported studies tried to construct ternary heterojunctions to realize the multi-step transfers of photogenerated carriers [36–38]. However, in this way, it is difficult to control accurately the transfer path and destination of photogenic electrons. Moreover, the single carrier driving force cannot extend lifetime of excitons.

Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been widely applied in photocatalysis because of the large work function and anti-Stokes luminescence. Due to the difference of Fermi energy levels, an energy barrier will be formed at the interface between CQD and semiconductor to prevent electronic backflow. It can also act as an active center of photocatalytic reactions [39–42]. There is no doubt that the quasi-ternary heterojunction photocatalysts constructed by CQD can not only induce multi-gradient transfer of charges, but also achieve precise control of the position of photogenerated electrons. However, the intrinsic relationship between the modification position of CQD, the transfer path of photogenerated carriers, and the catalytic activity has been rarely studied.

In this study, a 0D/1D/2D CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction has been constructed by thermal polymerization and hydrothermal treatment for efficient degradation of sulfadiazine (SDZ) pollutants under visible light. The recombination of heterojunction and CQD can realize the gradient transfers, in which exciton separation is further facilitated by dual carrier drive constructed by built-in electric field and energy barriers. This multi-gradient charge transfer mechanism can effectively prolong the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. Its electron transfer path was elucidated by contrast CdS/CQD/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, and the electron transfer mechanism was investigated by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and transient fluorescence spectroscopy (TRPL). In addition, the mechanisms of photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics in these systems were discussed by combining Gaussian calculation and experimental characterization. This work provides a reference for the application of multiple heterojunctions in photocatalysis in environmental remediation.

All reagents were purchased from Aladdin Reagent and their purity is above 98 %. All reagents are of analytical purity and do not require further purification. Deionized water (DW, 18.25 MΩ · cm) was utilized to experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bulk g-C3N4 and 2D g-C3N4 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by conventional thermal polymerization. In general, 10 g melamine (C3H6N6) was evenly dispersed at the bottom of the porcelain boat and then covered with a lid. At a heating rate of 5 °C/min, melamine was heated to 550 °C in a muffle furnace for 4 h and then cooled naturally.

2D g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by alkali intercalation with sonication. In general, 0.1 g bulk g-C3N4 and 0.1 g NaOH were dissolved in 10 mL DW and the dispersion was then stirred for 0.5 h. Next, the mixed solution was sonicated for 1 h. In the end, the mixed solution was centrifugated and washed with DW and anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH) followed by drying at 60 °C for 8 h in an oven.

In general, 5 g maltose and 7 mL 30 % H2O2 solution were added into a beaker and stirred to dissolve the reagents. The beaker was then placed in an oven for annealing for 20 min at 200 °C to obtain CQD appeared reddish-brown in color [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS nanorods, CQD/CdS and CQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: 4.62 g Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and 4.62 g thiourea (THU) were first added into 60 mL ethylenediamine (EDA). The mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h and then moved into a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon for hydrothermal reactions at 160 °C for 24 h. Finally, CdS nanorods were rinsed repeatedly with DW and C2H5OH until the solution became neutral, and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h in drying oven. CQD/CdS composite was prepared by impregnation method. In particular, 6 mg CQD and 60 mg CdS were dissolved in 10 mL DW and stirred for 3 h. CQD/g-C3N4 composite was obtained by the same method. The obtained products were denoted as CS (CQD/CDS composite) and CCN (CQD/g-C3N4 composite), respectively.

CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 nanocomposite was prepared by hydrothermal reactions. The prepared CQD/CdS mixed solution and 40 mg g-C3N4 were transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor at 160 °C for 3 h and then CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 was dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The resulting CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were denoted as CSCN. The synthetic process is shown in Fig. 1 . CdS/g-C3N4 (SCN) was also prepared by hydrothermal reactions of CdS and g-C3N4, while CdS/CQD/g-C3N4 (SCCN) was prepared from CdS and CCN by the same method. Unless otherwise specified, the ratio of CdS to g-C3N4 was maintained at 60 % for SCN, SCCN, and CSCN. CQD accounted for 6 % of the total in all samples in this study.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker) was utilized to analyze the crystal structure with Cu-Kα irradiation at 20 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Fisher) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI F20) were used to observe the morphology and microstructure, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to analyze the valence bonding of the prepared materials. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was obtained with the standard BaSO4 method with a Shimadzu UV2600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4700). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra were recorded with a transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS980). UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 60) was used to observe the concentration of Rh B, MB, and TC. The concentration of SDZ was determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The reaction pathway was analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Agilent QTOF). The work function and Fermi energy of each component were measured by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS).

The electrochemical and photoelectric sensing properties were tested using a traditional three-electrode system. In particular, 5 mg photocatalyst was ultrasonically dispersed in 1 mL isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and the dispersion (10 μL) was then transferred using a pipette onto conductive indium tin oxide (ITO, 10 × 20 × 1.2 mm) glass and dried at room temperature. The three-electrode system was immersed in an electrolyte with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the main component. A camera obscura with LED visible spotlights (45 W) with 420 nm as the cutoff wavelength was constructed to obtain the transient photocurrent response curves (TPRC) and Mott-Schottky (MS) curves. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was performed at scanning frequencies ranging from 100000 Hz to 0.01 Hz.

The photocatalytic degradation of Rh B, MB, hydrochloride (TC), and SDZ (15 mg/L) was carried out under the irradiation of a Xe lamp in a multistage photochemical reaction apparatus (CEL-LAB 500, CEAULIGHT) and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was evaluated. In the typical experiment, 30 mg photocatalyst was added to 50 mL solution to be degraded and stirred in the dark for 1 h in order to achieve an equilibrium of adsorption and desorption. After that, the Xenon lamp was turned on and the solution was taken every 30 min and centrifuged at 10000 rpm. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm needle filter. The UV–vis spectrophotometer and HPLC were used to detect changes in the concentration of Rh B, MB, TC, and SDZ. The C18 column is used for chromatographic separation and the column temperature was set and maintained at 30 °C. The isocratic mobile phase consisted of methanol and 0.1 % acetic acid with a ratio of 30:70 (V/V) and the flow velocity was 1 mL/min. The detection wavelength was 270 nm. In order to ensure reproducibility and reduce random errors, all photocatalytic tests were carried out three times or more.

Active species trapping experiments were carried out on all materials. The capturing agent (1 mM) used 3,3′-Dimethylbiphenyl-4,4′-diamine (DMB), EDTA-2Na, p-benzoquinone (BQ), IPA, l-Histidine (His) and AgNO3, which was introduced to detect H2O2, holes (h+), superoxide radicals (•O2 –), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), singlet oxygen molecule (1O2), and electronic (e-), respectively.

The production of H2O2 was determined by iodimetry. HCl solution, KI solution, and H2O2 solution were used to prepare the standard concentration of I3 - solution. The K value was obtained from ultraviolet spectrophotometry measurement according to the Lambert Beer law. In experiment 30 mg photocatalyst was mixed with 5 mL IPA and 45 mL water in a quartz test tube. 3 mL samples were taken at 30 min intervals and filtered by a microporous filter. After filtration, 1 mL potassium hydrogen phthalate solution (1 mol/L) and 1 mL KI solution (0.4 mol/L) were added to the sample and let stand for 30 min. The absorption intensity of I3 - was measured by a UV spectrophotometer and the concentration of H2O2 was calculated.

HPLC-MS was used to analyze the intermediate products of SDZ during the degradation process. The parameter setting of HPLC was the same as the above experiments. In the full scan mode of MS, the parameter range of m/z was set as 50–500, the capillary voltage was 3 kV, the cone voltage was 30 V, and the solvent removal temperature was set as 350 °C.

TEM and HRTEM were used to reveal the microstructure of the synthesized materials. Fig. 2 a shows clearly that the particle size distribution of uniformly dispersed CQD ranged from 2.5 to 6.0 nm. with an average particle size of 5.20 nm (histogram is given as inset). The lattice fringes of 0.218 nm in spacing (Fig. 2b) of a CQD correspond to the (100) crystal lattice planes of graphitic carbon [43]. Identification of 1D CdS and 2D g-C3N4 (Fig. 2c-d and Fig. S1) showed successful synthesis of these structures. As shown in Fig. 2e, CQD were successfully loaded onto the CdS nanorods. The characteristic crystal lattice plane spacings of 0.221 nm and 0.338 nm are indicated in the HRTEM image given in Fig. 2f, which correspond to the (100) lattice planes of CQD and (002) lattice planes of CdS, respectively. Fig. 2g showed the layered structure of g-C3N4. Through the amplification of the two-phase interface (Fig. 2h), it can be seen that CdS is successfully combined with g-C3N4, and the close binding of the two is conducive to the rapid transmission of photoelectric electrons in the two-phase heterojunction. The atomic-level tessellation of CQD and CdS creates the necessary conditions for the generation of the energy barrier. And the indices of crystal face were calibrated. The morphology and structure of CSCN and SCCN, as shown in Fig. 2i-2 l, showed tightly bonded interfaces, which should accelerate the formation of the built-in electric field in the heterojunctions. In addition, the elemental mapping images displayed in Fig. 2m and 2 m1-4 showed the presence and distribution of C, N, S, and Cd. The EDX analysis further confirmed that CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 had been successfully synthesized.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the characteristic diffraction peaks due to pure g-C3N4 (PDF#87–1526) were observed at 13.2° (110) and 27.8° (002). The characteristic diffraction peaks due to pure CdS were observed at 24.8°, 26.4°, 28.1°, 43.6°, 47.7°, and 51.7°, corresponding to (001), (002), (101), (110), (103) and (112) crystal lattice planes, respectively (PDF#75–1545). According to the XRD pattern of CQD (Fig. S2), a typical broad peak at 19.3° was observed, which is related to the (002) reflections of amorphous graphitic carbon. According to Debye-Scherrer formula, the interplanar spacing of CQD is estimated to be 0.46 nm, which is much greater than the (002) lattice spacing of graphite (0.33 nm). This is because a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups exist at the edge of CQD that would counterbalance the interlayer van der Waals forces between graphitic layers, which nonetheless could help transferring electrons into the solution [13]. The XRD patterns of CSCN and SCCN appeared the same as that of CdS, indicating that the contributions from CQD and g-C3N4 were negligible relative to CdS. The reason is the amorphous character of g-C3N4 and the low content of CQD.

Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of CQD, CdS, g-C3N4, and their composites. The CQD absorption peaks at 3443, 2919, 1718, 1616, 1415, and 1028 cm−1 represent OH, CH, CO, CC, COC, and CO bonds, respectively. The absorption peaks at 3410 and 1669 cm−1 are due to the water molecules in CdS. The absorption peaks at 1388 and 1100 cm−1 are assigned to the Cd-S bands. In the spectra of g-C3N4, the characteristic peaks at 1635, 1559, 1427, 1317, and 1232 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration modes of the heterocyclic structures and the peak at 809 cm−1 is ascribed to the breathing mode of the s-triazine structure of g-C3N4. The broad peaks at 3441 and 3254 cm−1 are due to the OH and NH bonds, originated from the absorption of water molecules. The spectra of CSCN and SCCN are similar to those of g-C3N4 and no characteristic peaks due to CdS and CQD were observed because the concentration of CQD was low and the peaks due to CdS overlapped with the strong peak due to CN vibrations. Nonetheless. the results showed that the graphitized structure of g-C3N4 could be retained well after compositing.

The surface electronic states of SCN, CSCN, and SCCN composites were characterized by XPS, which revealed clearly the presence of C, N, Cd, S, and O (Fig. S4). After processing the graph, four characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286.5, 288.0, and 288.5 eV for SCN were identified in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c), which were due to CC, CC, CO, and N-CN, respectively. In contrast, the CC peak for SCCN and CSCN shifted to a lower binding energy. This is due to an increase of the local electron density in g-C3N4 when CQD were introduced. Compared with SCCN, the chemical shift for CSCN was more profound. This indicates that the density of local electron cloud was higher in CSCN and the electron activity was greater. Fig. 3d shows the N 1 s spectrum, in which the two characteristic peaks at 398.6 and 399.7 eV are attributed to triazine structure-N (C-NC) and tertiary nitrogen-N (N-(C)3), respectively. The three nitrogen units constitute the basic g-C3N4 structural unit. The peaks due to Cd 3d at 404.5 and 411.3 eV (Fig. 3e) for SCN correspond to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively. As for CSCN, the corresponding peaks shifted towards lower binding energy compared to SCN. Surprisingly, the Cd 3d peaks for SCCN shifted towards high binding energy, which means that it has a lower surface electron density. These results indicate that the role of CQD in SCCN is quite different from that of CSCN. In SCCN, the binding energy of Cd 3d shifted to higher binding energy and the CC peak showed a positive shift to lower binding energy. These changes are attributed to the difference in Fermi energy of CdS and g-C3N4. CdS has a higher Fermi energy, so electrons flow from CdS to g-C3N4, which causes a change in electron density on both sides. The electron density increases on the g-C3N4 side, while it decreases on the CdS side. However, in CSCN, both CC and Cd 3d binding energies were reduced, which is due to the higher Fermi energy of CdS that enabled intrinsic carrier transfers from CdS to CQD when CQD recombined with CdS. Since the Fermi level of g-C3N4 is the lowest, after recombining g-C3N4, the intrinsic carriers will be spontaneously transferred from CdS to g-C3N4. In Fig. 4 f, the S 2p peaks at 161.0 and 162.1 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, demonstrating the existence of S2- [44]. As shown in Fig. S5 (O 1 s spectrum), the characteristic peaks at 531.6 eV (CO) and 533.3 eV (–OH) were observed and their occurrence is attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups in CQD. Compared with SCCN, the binding energy of O 1 s in CSCN is lower. This is attributed to the different electron densities of CQD in SCCN and CSCN. Due to the dual driving forces of the built-in electric field and the potential energy barrier, electrons in CSCN eventually would flow to CQD and the electron density of CQD would increase, resulting in lower binding energy. Both effects were verified with the C 1 s spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric chemical properties and energy band structure
ce:section.ce:para: The blue fluorescence of CQD at 365 nm is attributed to its quantum effect as shown in Fig. 4a, while the UV absorption spectrum demonstrates the π-π* transition due to the presence of a delocalized large π bonds [13]. The synergy of quantum effect and conjugation effect of delocalized π bonds is powerful for binding electrons. The PL spectra of CQD (Fig. 4b) confirmed shorter fluorescence wavelengths can be obtained by excitation of visible or infrared light with longer wavelengths. Besides providing energy barriers and active sites, CQD also played an important role in broadening the absorption band of heterojunctions.

From the UV–vis DRS shown in Fig. 4c, the absorption edges of g-C3N4 and CdS are 460 nm and 527 nm, respectively. For SCN, SCCN and CSCN, two absorption edges appeared because these composites were composed of CdS and g-C3N4. Compared with other materials, the introduction of CQD broadened the absorption band at 500–700 nm for CSCN and SCCN, which shows the capacity of full spectral absorption was enhanced because of the anti-Stokes effect of CQD. Fig. 4d shows the band gap values measured by the Tauc plot of g-C3N4, CdS, CSCN, SCN, and SCCN with band gap of 2.79, 2.39, 2.41, 2.43, and 2.42 eV, respectively. The conduction band (CB) potential was measured by the Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. 4e). According to the Kubelka-Munk theory, the CB potentials vs Ag/AgCl of CdS and g-C3N4 were calculated to be −0.85 and −0.8 eV, respectively. The potential values of Ag/AgCl are subsequently converted to the NHE scale using Eq. (1) [45].

N H E , p H = 7

Therefore, the CB potentials of CdS and g-C3N4 were obtained as −0.65 and −0.6 eV vs NHE (-3.85 and −3.8 eV vs Vac). Combined with the experimental measurement of the band gap, the valence bands (VB) of g-C3N4 and CdS were calculated as 2.19 and 1.74 eV vs NHE (-6.59 and −6.24 eV vs Vac), respectively. The VB-XPS spectrum (Fig. S6) shows that the VB of SCCN and CSCN are 1.74 and 1.71 eV vs NHE (-6.24 and −6.21 eV vs Vac), respectively.

UPS can be used to analyze the ability of each component to capture electrons and determine the relationship between energy band structure and vacuum (Vac) [46]. As shown in Fig. 4f, the work functions of CdS, g-C3N4 and CQD are 3.95, 4.29 and 4.08 eV, respectively. Thus, their Fermi energy levels were calculated as −3.95, −4.29 and −4.08 eV vs Vac. Surprisingly, the work function of CQD is slightly lower than that of g-C3N4. This means that the electrons spontaneously dispersed towards g-C3N4 when CQD was attached to g-C3N4 in SCCN. In order to know the precise electron transfer path of SCCN and CSCN, it is necessary to analyze the band structure and the built-in electric field of the material. As shown in Fig. 4g, the valence band (VB) positions of each component were calculated from UPS data [47]. Combined with their Fermi energy levels, the VB positions of CdS, g-C3N4, and CQD are −6.2, −6.13 and −5.46 eV vs Vac, respectively. Fig. 4h shows the band structure of each component. After contact, several interior electric fields were built when the ternary composites were formed. After g-C3N4 is in contact with CdS, due to the difference in Fermi level, it can be judged from the results of XPS that intrinsic carriers flow from CdS to g-C3N4. When an equilibrium is reached, an electric double layer is formed at the interface of the heterojunction, in which the positive (negative) charges are accumulated on the CdS (g-C3N4) side. These charges in the electric double layer generate a built-in electric field directed from CdS to g-C3N4, thereby driving the directional transfers of carriers. Therefore, when the electrons are transitioned by illumination, especially when the photogenerated carriers in the heterojunction flow from the CB of g-C3N4 to the CB of CdS, the backflow of electrons is prevented due to the existence of the built-in electric field. In SCCN (Fig. 4i), when CQD is introduced into g-C3N4, the electrons are forced to break through the binding of quantum effect and conjugate effect of CQD and transfer to g-C3N4 due to the greater work function of g-C3N4. In the heterojunction between CdS and g-C3N4, electrons will spontaneously transfer from the CB of g-C3N4 to the CB of CdS under illumination. The back-migration of photogenerated electrons is hindered by the built-in electric field. Compared with SCN, more photogenerated electrons will be accumulated in CdS. However, due to the lack of active sites on CdS, a large number of photogenerated electrons cannot exist stably. Moreover, excessive photoelectrons will aggravate the photo-corrosion of CdS [48,49], which will greatly reduce the photocatalytic activity of SCCN. But for CSCN (Fig. 4j), photogenerated electrons are first transferred from g-C3N4 to CdS, and they are also hard to backflow under the action of the built-in electric field between g-C3N4 and CdS. Because of the lower Fermi energy levels of CQD, the electrons spontaneously transfer to the CQD until the Fermi level flattens out. When equilibrium is reached, an electric double layer is formed at the interface between CdS and CQD with positive charges on the side of CdS and negative charges on the side of CQD. The near-surface region of CdS has a lower concentration of free charges than the bulk, thus forming a space charge region in which electrons dissipate to form a charge depletion layer. Electrons will migrate in this region, leading to band bending of CdS. The difference in Fermi levels will lead to the formation of an energy barrier at the interface, which will block the recombination of electrons and holes. The synergistic effect of the built-in electric field and the energy barrier enables CSCN to achieve electron gradient transfers. Moreover, nanoscale CQD can also be used as an excellent active site to improve the photocatalytic reaction rate. Therefore, the heterojunction type of CdS and g-C3N4 is not the traditional type Ⅱ heterojunction. For CSCN, photogenerated electrons are transferred from g-C3N4 with low CB to the VB of CdS, not transferred from the high CB to the low CB (type Ⅱ), which is in line with the conditions for forming Z scheme heterojunction. Combined with the band structure of CdS and g-C3N4, it is inferred that CSCN forms a new Z-scheme heterojunction.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy was used to determine the photogenerated charge separation and transfer rates. As shown in Fig. 5 a, under the excitation condition of 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CSCN is much lower than that of g-C3N4 and SCCN because the multi-gradient transfer of electrons under the built-in electric field and the energy level barrier inhibits the recombination of electron-holes. Furthermore, in order to obtain the lifetime of photogenerated carriers, transient fluorescence spectroscopy was applied and results are shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence lifetime curves of photogenerated carriers are all fitted with a double exponential function in the form.

The average carrier life of g-C3N4, SCCN and CSCN are 6.71, 7.55, and 9.34 ns, respectively. These results further indicate that multigradient electron transfers prolonged greatly the carrier’s lifetime. Under driving and capture of electrons by the heterojunction and CQD, the photogenerated carriers enter the active sites smoothly in CSCN. At the same time, the built-in electric field and the energy barrier greatly promote exciton separations and inhibit electron-hole annihilation. However, CQD lose the ability to bind electrons in SCCN, which is not enough to prolong the life of photogenerated carriers. Fig. 5c shows the transient photocurrent responses upon irradiation. SCN, SCCN, and CSCN presents more photocurrent density compared with CdS and g-C3N4, attributed to the heterointerface interactions. It can be seen that SCCN has the strongest photocurrent density. This is because electrons are transferred by multiple gradients and eventually accumulate in CdS, which, as an excellent photosensitizer, is easier to convert optical signals into electrical ones. For CSCN, a large number of electrons eventually transfers to CQD, making fewer electrons to participate in photoelectric signal conversion. In addition, only the photocurrent from CSCN is stable after several cycles because the photo-corrosion is greatly reduced after the photogenerated carrier breaks away from CdS [51]. Fig. 5d shows that the radius of curvature in Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data of CSCN is the smallest, which means that the electron transfer resistance in the interface is minimum. This also explains that CSCN has the strongest electron transfer capability. Therefore, the highest separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carrier can be obtained by adjusting the position of CQD in the heterojunction.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared catalysts to degrade Rh B, MB, SDZ, and TC were tested. Using the degradation of Rh B and MB, the optimal ratio of CSCN photocatalytic performance was explored, and according to the photocatalytic data of Rh B, the mass ratios of CQD in the CSCN composite was determined, which have an effect on the photodegradation performance (Fig. S7-S9). Fig. S10 shows the adsorption capacity of all samples. Compared with photocatalysis, the effect of dark adsorption on pollutant concentration was less than 30 % for all samples. Fig. 6 a shows the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of Rh B in 180 min by CdS and g-C3N4 are 11.62 % and 13.48 % respectively. The low degradation efficiency of single-component material is due to the rapid annihilation of excitons. It can be seen that a higher degradation efficiency of SCN (84.82 %) was obtained. This is due to the unique built-in electric field in the heterojunction which facilitates the separation of electrons and holes. It is surprising that the photocatalytic performance of SCCN (38.51 %), which has a large number of photogenerated electrons (Fig. 5c), is far poorer than that of SCN. Because the accumulation of a large number of photogenerated electrons on CdS leads to serious photo-corrosion, thus reducing the photocatalytic performance. For CSCN, the highest degradation efficiency was obtained (94.67 %) due to the dual action of built-in electric field and the energy level barrier. Photogenerated electrons are eventually transferred to CQD, which greatly slows down the photo-corrosion in CdS. In addition, CQD can also be used as the active site to further improve the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 6b, the relationship between irradiation time and ln (C0/Ct) is almost linear, indicating that the degradation process of Rh B conforms to quasi-first-order kinetics. The k value of CSCN (0.01363 min−1) for the degradation of Rh B is 18.75, 16.64, 1.35, and 5.43 times that of CdS (0.000727 min−1), CN (0.000819 min−1), SCN (0.01012 min−1), and SCCN (0.00251 min−1), respectively.

According to the cyclic experiments shown in Fig. 6c, the degradation performance of CSCN was almost unchanged after repeated degradation of Rh B for five times, indicating that CSCN had good photodegradation stability. No changes were observed in XRD patterns after 5 cycles (in Fig. 6f), demonstrating that CSCN has a good structural stability. In addition, the data of atomic absorption spectroscopy confirmed that the content of Cd2+ in aqueous solution was very low (0.005980 mg/kg), which was lower than the national standard. This further enhances the potential of the material for practical applications. And the TEM，FTIR and XPS patterns images of CSCN photocatalyst before and after the photocatalysis are presented in Fig. S11, which the structure and valence bonds do not change significantly. To test the degradation performance of the catalyst for antibiotics, SDZ was degraded by using the same material. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, the degradation efficiency of g-C3N4, CdS, SCN, SCCN and CSCN are 9.60 %, 57.99 %, 48.67 %, 51.54 %, and 75.99 %, and the kinetic constants (Fig. 6e) are 0.00056, 0.00484, 0.00291, 0.00368, and 0.00755 min−1, respectively. CdS is not stable in water, and it is easy to release trace Cd2+, which complexes with SDZ, thus causing the decrease of SDZ concentration for the most part. Similarly, CSCN has the best degradation efficiency and degradation rate in the degradation of antibiotics. In order to analyze the universality of catalytic degradation, other pollutants are degraded (in Fig. S12), and the higher degradation efficiency shows that CSCN has a wide range of degradation properties. In addition, as shown in Fig. S13, the data of photocatalytic degradation of CSCN were analyzed by ANOVA.

In order to explore the photocatalytic mechanism, radical quenching experiment was carried out. Different radical quenchers including EDTA-2Na (h+), BQ (•O2 –), IPA (•OH), His (1O2), AgNO3 (e-), and DMB (H2O2) were added in the degradation experiment of CSCN. As displayed in Fig. 7 a, the capacity of degrading Rh B decreased significantly after the addition of DMB, BQ, and His. EDTA-2Na had a moderate effect on the degradation. However, the photocatalytic efficiency was almost unaffected by IPA and AgNO3. We found that the main active species of CSCN were H2O2, •O2 – and 1O2. Combined with the energy band structure of CSCN, the CB potential (-0.65 eV vs NHE) is more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2 – (-0.33 eV vs NHE) and its VB value (1.76 eV vs NHE) is smaller than that of H2O/•OH (2.27 eV vs NHE), hence CSCN could produce •O2 – directly instead of •OH in the catalytic process·H2O2 as the main reaction radical can be obtained directly by acquisition of an electron of oxygen due to the smaller redox potential of O2/H2O2 (0.281 eV vs NHE) and it then decomposes to obtain •OH. In addition, •O2 – may also be converted to H2O2 because its redox potential is 0.895 eV vs NHE [52]. For 1O2, the potential of O2/1O2 is 1.88 eV vs NHE. The VB potential of the CSCN of the type Ⅱ heterojunction is 1.74 eV vs NHE, which is not sufficient to convert O2 to 1O2. The CSCN of heterojunction of Z scheme has more oxidizing VB potential (2.19 eV vs NHE). The experimental results confirm that CSCN has Z-scheme heterojunctions. In order to further understand the photocatalytic mechanism of the composite photocatalyst system, free radical trapping experiments were conducted on SCN and SCCN and the inhibition rates of various free radical quenchers on degradation properties are shown in Fig. 7b. It can be seen that the main active species of SCN are H2O2, •O2 – and 1O2, while that of SCCN are •O2 – and 1O2 [53]. The removal of H2O2 has a stronger inhibition effect on CSCN, indicating that CQD, as the final destination of photogenerated electrons, help to generate H2O2. In addition, the iodine volume method detects the output of H2O2 (Fig. S10) and further proves the promotion effect of CQD in catalytic generation of H2O2. And the Mott-Schottky plot of the composite was tested (Fig. S15). The flat band potential of the composite material is above the potential generated by H2O2, so the results further proved the possibility of photocatalytic H2O2 production.

The active species of SDZ were also analyzed. As can be seen from Fig. 7d, H2O2 is still one of the most active species. In addition, compared with the degradation process of Rh B, the degree of inhibition for h+, •OH, and e- to degradation reaction was significantly increased, while that for •O2 – and 1O2 diminished slightly. This indicates that different kinds of free radicals have different offensive and oxidation–reduction capacities for various pollutants. The main active species of SDZ degraded by SCN, SCCN, and CSCN were •O2 – and H2O2, 1O2, h+ and H2O2, respectively (Fig. 7e). Compared with the inhibition rate of •O2 – among the three different materials, the introduction of CQD can reduce its content and impact, and promote the conversion of •O2 – into H2O2. The separation of exciton in CSCN also leads to the accumulation of h+. For SCCN, different carrier transfer paths accelerate the accumulation of more photogenerated electrons and holes, and a large number of h+ facilitate the conversion of O2 to 1O2. The results of EPR data (Fig. 7c and f) further indicate that the main active free radicals produced by CSCN are •O2 – and 1O2. In dark conditions, almost no signal was generated, but with the onset of light excitation, the •O2 – and 1O2 signal curves began to appear, and the density gradually increased over time. Thus, the EPR curve further confirmed the generation of active species during photocatalytic degradation, which continued to produce with prolonged illumination time. In addition, the strong signal of 1O2 provides further evidence that CSCN has Z-scheme heterojunctions but not type Ⅱ heterojunctions. If electrons were transferred to g-C3N4, given the hole motion, they would accumulate in CdS. In this case, the positive potential of the hole was less than E (O2/1O2 = 1.88 eV vs NHE), so it is not feasible to use the characteristics of the type Ⅱ heterojunction to produce 1O2. In view of the above experimental results, various methods for generation of free radical were established accordingly. Photogenerated electrons of CSCN convert O2 dissolved in water to •O2 – [Eq. (5)] and H2O2 [Eq. (6)], respectively. As shown in Eq. (7), •O2 – also reacts with e- to convert to H2O2. Partial H2O2 instability will break down a little bit of •OH in Eq. (8). As shown in Eq. (9), h+ will absorb electrons from •O2 –, which becomes 1O2. Finally, various types of pollutants are degraded into pollution-free substances under the synergistic action of •O2 –, 1O2, H2O2, and •OH.

In order to further understand the degradation process of pollutants, the reaction sites were calculated by Gaussian software and the intermediates were analyzed by LC-MS. According to the high content of m/z and the literature, the reasonable degradation path of SDZ was deduced. SDZ was used as the representatives of antibiotic pollutants. Theoretical calculations were carried out to identify the primary reactive sites by using B3LYP method with 6–311 + G (d, p) basis set in Gaussian 16. The optimized structure and atomic numbering of SDZ are depicted in Fig. 8 a. Atom charges of SDZ were calculated by the Multiwfn program (Table S1) and its electrostatic potential maps were also analyzed (Fig. 8b). Naturally, an atom with more positive (negative) atomic charge is more likely to be attacked by nucleophilic (electrophilic) groups. Based on the computational results, the potential reactive functional groups are -SO2-, –NH2, –NH- and pyrimidine-N. The mass to charge ratio (m/z) of the intermediates were measured by LC-MS, and the corresponding chemical formula are shown in Table S2. According to the results of theoretical calculations and experiment, the possible degradation path of SDZ is proposed as follows. As shown in Fig. 8c, there are four main attack routes of free radicals, namely the pyrimidine ring, aniline ring, AOP, and sulfanilamide key. When the pyrimidine ring was attacked by the free radicals, a hydroxylation product (S1) is first formed. Under the attack of •OH, the rupture of the CN bond on the pyrimidine ring leads to the formation of S2. The free radical nucleophilic attack on C atoms near S leads to the formation of S3. The rupture of the SC bond and the oxidation of –NH2 result in the formation of S4. The radicals further attack the nitro group, forming S5 eventually. The free radical attack path on aniline ring is relatively simple. The formation of S6 is attributed to the oxidation of an amide group to form nitro group on the benzene ring of SDZ. Then, the CN bond on the aniline ring is broken and forms S7. Afterwards, SN dissociates to form S8. The presence of S9 and S10 indicated that -SO2- was extruded during degradation. This is one oft-reported way in which SDZ is degraded by AOP processes [54]. The formation of S9 can be attributed to hydroxylation of reactive amino groups. On this basis, S9 rearranges atoms to form S10. The –NH- and –NH2 group on S10 can be oxidized by direct electron transfer and free radical attack to form S4. And then it converts to S5. The sulfanilamide key-based attack is mainly due to the extremely high electro-positivity of the S atom (Table S1). Nucleophilic reactions are more prone to occur here. SN dissociates to form S11 and S12, and they are converted to S13 and S8 respectively under the further attack by •OH. The CN bond of S12 can be easily broken under the attack of •O2 – and the intermediate is further decomposed into the ring-opening product S14. The -SO3H on S13 is unstable and it becomes S5 after removal. The total ion current maps at different time points were summarized in SI (Fig. S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for photogenerated charge transfer and catalytic reactions
ce:section.ce:para: The above experimental results show that it is feasible to induce electron multi-gradient transfers and prolong exciton lifetime by constructing an internal electric field and energy barrier. Scheme 1 a1 shows the photoelectron-generating mechanism of CdS/g-C3N4 catalyst. Only the built-in electric field is involved in the induced photoelectron transfer. Photogenerated electrons are transferred to CdS and photocatalytic reactions occur on the CdS surface.

In Scheme 1a2, the electrons are transferred from CQD to g-C3N4 by the difference in work function for SCCN and the built-in electric field drives a large number of electrons into CdS. At this point, the catalyst is similar to SCN, but the accumulated excessive photogenerated electrons do not necessarily promote the photocatalytic reactions. The occurrence of photo-corrosion for CdS results in the reduction of photocatalytic degradation performance.

For CSCN (Scheme 1a3), photogenerated electrons are initially driven into CdS by the built-in electric field in CdS/g-C3N4 heterojunctions. Since CQD has a higher work function than CdS, the electrons are further transferred to CQD. Quantum and conjugate effects of CQD can store electrons in large quantities. Then, an energy barrier appears between CQD and CdS due to the difference of Fermi energy, which further inhibits electronic backflow and the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The combination of CQD and CdS not only provides many active sites, but also greatly reduces the accumulation of electrons on CdS, thus reducing the photo-corrosion of CdS and improving the photocatalytic ability.

Based on the experimental data and characterization, it’s proved that the heterojunction type of CSCN is not the traditional type Ⅱ heterojunction (Scheme 1b1). The Z-scheme heterojunction structure of CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 and potential diagrams of related free radicals (Scheme 1b2) were constructed. Under visible light irradiation, CdS and g-C3N4 monomeric materials in CSCN form Z-scheme heterojunction and generate exciton states. Due to the built-in electric field and the characteristics of the Z-scheme heterojunction, excitons are separated into CdS to form photogenerated carriers. And the difference in work function further induces the transfer of carriers to CQD. In addition, the interception of photogenerated carriers in CQD is realized under the energy barrier. The photocatalytic mechanism and charge transfer route in CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 are proposed (Scheme 1c). It can be seen that in the multi-step charge transfer process, the electron experiences the longest transfer path and activates the related free radicals in CQD and finally completes the photocatalytic reaction.

In summary, two quasi-ternary photocatalysts with dual carrier driving force, CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 and CdS/CQD/g-C3N4, have been designed and synthesized under the guidance of the induced multi-step charge transfer strategy. It is proved that the heterojunction type of CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 is Z-scheme heterojunction. CQD/CdS/g-C3N4 had the strongest photocatalytic degradation performance and the removal rates of Rh B and SDZ were 94.7 % and 76.0 %, which were 2.46 times and 1.47 times of CdS/CQD/g-C3N4, respectively. The work function of CQD is between CdS and g-C3N4, making different electron transfer paths by changing the attachment position of CQD in the catalyst. For CQD/CdS/g-C3N4, the photogenerated electrons transferred from g-C3N4 to CdS and then to CQD, while they went from CQD to g-C3N4 and then to CdS for CdS/CQD/g-C3N4. Photogenerated carriers realize multi-gradient transfers, which is the result of dual driving by built-in electric field and energy barriers. It was found that CQD as the active sites could effectively promote the production of H2O2 and the EPR signal provided strong evidence for the existence of •O2 – and 1O2. In addition, the free radical transformation process and four degradation paths of sulfadiazine were explored. This study provides a novel strategy to the design of efficient and stable photocatalysts for environmental pollution treatment.
